claims.evidence_text: work out harvest index (HI) as grain divided by total. A very good crop should have a value of about 0.5 i.e. 50 percent of the crops above-ground weight should be grain... If early growth was excessive and water ran out for grain filling, HI could fall to 30 percent or less.
claims.claim_summary: A very good wheat crop has harvest index ~0.5 (50% grain); water shortage during grain fill can drop it to 30% or less.

claims.evidence_text: Check average kernel weight in the different treatments by taking a handful of grain... If any weights are much below 3 g [per 100 kernels], the treatment was short of water during grain filling.
claims.claim_summary: Wheat 100-kernel weight much below 3 g (i.e. TKW below ~30 g) indicates water shortage during grain filling.

claims.evidence_text: A review of over 30 tillage trials found that tillage method (conventional or zero-till) did not change yield when data were averaged across studies, locations and years. However, in some places and some years either method could be far better (+1 t ha-1) or far worse (-1 t ha-1).
claims.claim_summary: Across 30+ trials wheat yield was unchanged on average between conventional and zero tillage, but varied +/-1 t/ha by site and year.

claims.evidence_text: erosion can be stopped on 15 percent slopes if the surface is covered by straw (about 4 t ha-1 is needed)... If left on the surface, residues will act as a mulch to keep soil surface temperature down and conserve moisture.
claims.claim_summary: Crop residue (~4 t/ha straw) suppresses erosion on slopes and mulches soil, but can delay emergence in cool areas and tie up nitrogen.
